coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical sensitive detection of insulin based on CdS/polydopamine co-sensitized WO3 nanorod and signal amplification of carbon nanotubes@polydopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Rongyu
Ma, Hongmin
Zhang, Yong
Wang, Qi
Yang, Zhongping
Du, Bin
Wu, Dan
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical sandwich immunosensor was designed for detection of insulin based on WO3/CdS/polydopamine (WO3/CdS/PDA) co-sensitized and PDA@carbon nanotubes (PDA@CNT) conjugates for signal amplification. The CdS nanoparticles were first deposited on the WO3 nanorods via sequential chemical bath deposition to form the WO3/CdS structure to enhance photocurrent. Then equipped with PDA to form the WO3/CdS/PDA photosensitive structure. The PDA was used not only to reduce the toxicity of CdS but also adsorb insulin primary antibodies (Ab1). Meanwhile, insulin secondary antibodies (Ab2) were decorated by PDA@CNT conjugates for signal amplification and further enhance photocurrent. Different photocurrent intensities were obtained by the photoelectrochemical workstation at applied bias of 0V due to the different amount of the PDA@CNT conjugates introduced by the different concentrations of insulin. A good linear relationship was obtained between the increased photocurrent and insulin concentrations range from 0.01ngmL−1 to 50ngmL−1. And a detection limit of 2.8pgmL−1 was obtained. The proposed sensor was applied to the determination of the insulin in human serum sample, and satisfactory results were obtained. The sensor presented good specificity, reproducibility and stability, thus it might find application in the clinical diagnosis of insulin or other biomarkers in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydopamine
Insulin
Photoelectrochemical
Layer-by-layer nano-assembly
Sandwich sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the PEC immunosensor via lay-by-lay method
SEM Characterization of the ITO/WO3/CdS/PDA electrode
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization of WO3 and CdS
The optimization of PEC detection conditions
Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM images of (A) CNTs (B) PDA@CNT and (c) the FTIR spectra of PDA@CNT, CNT and PDA.
The SEM images of (A) WO3 (B) WO3/CdS (C) WO3/CdS/PDA respectively and (D) the EDS spectrum of the WO3/CdS electrode.
The XPS images of (A) WO3 (B) WO3/CdS.
(A) Effect of WO3 concentrations; (B) The effect of CdS coating numbers on photocurrent intensity of the ITO/WO3 electrode; (C) The effect of pH influence. Errors bar=RSD (n=3).

(A) Photocurrent responses of (a) the ITO electrode, (b) WO3 coated on the electrode, (c) CdS deposited on the electrode, (d) PDA subsequently coating, (e) Ab1 immobilization, (f) BSA blocking, (g) incubation with 10μL of 1ngmL−1 Ag, (h) further incubation with PDA@CNT@Ab2 conjugates; (B) Corresponding Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy; (C) The principle of this work; (D) Photocurrent responses of the PEC sensor with different concentrations of insulin (ΔI, ΔI=I 0-I, I 0 is the photocurrent response of the PEC sensor without antigen and I is the photocurrent response of solution containing antigen). Errors bar=RSD (n=3).
Schematic illustration of the PEC sandwich transducer.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical sensitive detection of insulin based on CdS/polydopamine co-sensitized WO3 nanorod and signal amplification of carbon nanotubes@polydopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical sandwich immunosensor was designed for detection of insulin based on WO3/CdS/polydopamine (WO3/CdS/PDA) co-sensitized and PDA@carbon nanotubes (PDA@CNT) conjugates for signal amplification. The CdS nanoparticles were first deposited on the WO3 nanorods via sequential chemical bath deposition to form the WO3/CdS structure to enhance photocurrent. Then equipped with PDA to form the WO3/CdS/PDA photosensitive structure. The PDA was used not only to reduce the toxicity of CdS but also adsorb insulin primary antibodies (Ab1). Meanwhile, insulin secondary antibodies (Ab2) were decorated by PDA@CNT conjugates for signal amplification and further enhance photocurrent. Different photocurrent intensities were obtained by the photoelectrochemical workstation at applied bias of 0V due to the different amount of the PDA@CNT conjugates introduced by the different concentrations of insulin. A good linear relationship was obtained between the increased photocurrent and insulin concentrations range from 0.01ngmL−1 to 50ngmL−1. And a detection limit of 2.8pgmL−1 was obtained. The proposed sensor was applied to the determination of the insulin in human serum sample, and satisfactory results were obtained. The sensor presented good specificity, reproducibility and stability, thus it might find application in the clinical diagnosis of insulin or other biomarkers in the near future.

ce:section.ce:para: Insulin is an important protein hormone secreted by the pancreas islet beta cells and plays a key role as the predictor of diabetes and trauma (Melloul et al., 2002). Insulin participates in regulating glucose metabolism and controls the balance of the blood sugar (Melmed et al., 2011). If its concentration is lower than the normal levels (0.86ngmL−1), insulin could lead to a poor glucose metabolism and could further cause various kinds of diabetes mellitus (Dikow et al., 2009; Schutte et al., 2015). However, the concentration of insulin in human body is change frequently, and its change rate is another important approach for diagnosing diabetes mellitus. Therefore, quick and convenient detecting insulin is vital for clinical diagnosis.

Due to its great potential in future bio-analysis, photoelectrochemical (PEC) immunosensor with the features of low cost, high speed, easy miniaturization and high sensitivity (Haddour et al., 2006; Wang et al., 2017), has attracted substantial attention among the analytical chemistry community (Zhang et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2016b). In PEC detection, as the light illuminates the photoactive materials, an electrical signal can be detected (Ge et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2017). Due to the separation of exciter and detector, PEC immunosensors can get much higher sensitivity (Fan et al., 2014a; Wu et al., 2016; Yin et al., 2014). Besides, PEC active species (Li et al., 2017) and biological recognition elements are the two key factors for a desired PEC detector (Zhao et al., 2015).

To enhance the PEC signals, the method of coupling narrow band-gap semiconductors with wide band-gap semiconductors, which can increase the charge separation efficiency, has aroused more and more attention (Zhang et al., 2009). As one of the wide band-gap semiconductors, WO3 has been attracted much attention benefited from its high carrier mobility and chemical stability (Zhang and Liu, 2016). However, its utilization for PEC detector is still limited due to the lower conduction band (CB). So, coupling CdS with WO3 ( or other semiconductors, e.g. TiO2 and ZnO) is a common way that has been demonstrated (Fujishima et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016). The reason is that the band edges of CdS matched well with WO3, can facilitate the electron-hole pairs apart and thus reduce the chance of recombination (Zhang et al., 2014). However, as one of the most toxic heavy metals, when the cadmium compounds are prepared in nanocrystalline forms, the cadmium can be more dramatic to the environment (Bujňáková et al., 2017). So, to expand the application of cadmium compounds, several environmentally friendly materials have been used to composite with cadmium compounds for reducing the harmfulness, such as polyvinylpyrrolidon, graphere and L-cysteine (Kumar et al., 2017; Nisha et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015a).

Conventional methods for immobilization of biomolecules on matrix via using cross-linking agents, such as glutaraldehyde (Zhou et al., 2014a), 1-ethyl-3[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride /N-hydroxysuccinimide (Hou et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2011) and chitosan (Fan et al., 2014b), etc. However, using these agents were somewhat troublesome, such as tedious operation process, increasing operate time and high cost (Lee et al., 2008). Dopamine (DA) has the special structure like catechol and amine functional groups, which is similar to the adhesive proteins of mussels (Benes, 2001). More importantly, it is harmless to the environment. At pH=8.5, the DA molecules polymerized to form polydopamine (PDA) film that could be coated on solid substrates (Lee et al., 2007; Song et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2015b). Inspired by this phenomenon, a thin PDA film was synthesized via self-polymerization at pH=8.5 in this research. The as-fabricated PDA film was used not only as a cross-linker reagent for the immobilization of the insulin antibody but also as an electron donor. The quinone functional groups in the PDA are capable of covalent coupling to amine-terminated biomolecules (antibody) by Michael reactions (Cui et al., 2012; Gao et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2015). Thus, the high response signals can be successfully achieved, leading to the increment of stability or activity of the immobilized bio-components, which can also solve the problem of using cross-link agents. Furthermore, due to the good biocompatibility of PDA (Lin et al., 2014), the PDA functionalized CdS can reduce the toxicity and expand the scope of application of CdS.

In this paper, a PEC immunosensor for ultrasensitive detection of insulin was developed based on WO3/CdS/PDA co-sensitized structure and signal amplification of PDA@carbon nanotubes (PDA@CNT). The objective of this work is to develop a promising PDA film, which is used to immobilize biomolecules not only for the sensor matrix, but also for the signal labels. It is the first time for detection of insulin using this PEC method through the research of the recent literature. Meanwhile the constructed PEC transducer exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity, specificity and stability.

The PDA@CNT@Ab2 was synthesized based on the literature with a little changes (Fan et al., 2008). Shortly 50mL deionized water containing 50mg CNT, 100mg dopamine, and 60mg Tris was ultrasonic treatment in ice water bath for 1min. Then the solution was magnetically stirred at 30°C for 36h. Finally, the PDA@CNT was obtained after filtered and washed three times. Then 2mg PDA@CNT was dispersed into 1mL detection insulin antibodies (Ab2) solution. The mixed suspension was shaking at 4°C incubating for 12h and then the unbound Ab2 was removed by successive centrifugation. Finally, the resulting PDA@CNT@Ab2 were obtained and dispersed in 1mL 0.1M of PBS (pH 7.4).

For preparation, the ITO electrodes were cut into 2.5×0.6cm2 pieces and pre-processing according to the previous literature (Liu et al., 2014) and detected the electric side to use as the working electrode.

The WO3 nanorods were prepared based on the literature reported with a little changes (Gu et al., 2006). Typically, 1g Na2WO4·2H2O and 0.2g NaCl were added in 30mL ultrapure water to stir for 6h. Then pH value of the solution was adjusted to around 2 with concentrated hydrochloric acid and stirred for another 3h. Then, the solution was heated to 180°C for 24h in a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Finally, the precipitate was centrifuged and washed then dried at 60°C. Finally the WO3 nanorods were obtained.

10μL of the certain concentration of WO3 suspension was dropped on the electric side of the ITO electrode and the area was about 0.2cm2. After the WO3 film drying in air, it was calcined at 450°C for 30min and cooled to room temperature naturally. Finally it was referred as ITO/WO3 electrode. The varied concentrations of WO3 suspension were among 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0 and 3.5mgmL−1 to obtain different thickness WO3 modified electrode.

The ITO/WO3/CdS electrode was obtained via sequential chemical bath deposition method (Vogel et al., 1994). Shortly, the ITO/WO3 electrode was dipped into [Cd(NH3)4]2+ aqueous solution (6mL concentrated ammonia was dropped into 24mL, 0.1MCd(NO3)2 aqueous solution) and 0.1M Na2S for 30s respectively, every step was washed with ultrapure water. The coating step was repeated several times as needed.

Then the electrode obtained above was immersed into 3mgmL−1 dopamine Tris-HCl solution (5M, pH 8.5) stored in the 4°C refrigerator for 12h (Wan et al., 2011). Finally, the obtained ITO/WO3/CdS/PDA electrodes chip were washed by flow water and stored in the 4°C refrigerator for the following fabrication of immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the PEC immunosensor via lay-by-lay method
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the existence of the PDA, the capture insulin antibodies (Ab1) could attach to the ITO/WO3/CdS/PDA electrode without any treatment. In short, 10μL of Ab1 (10μgmL−1) was dropped onto the ITO/WO3/CdS/PDA electrode. After incubated at 4°C, it was rinsed thoroughly to remove the unbound and physically absorbed Ab1. In order to block nonspecific binding sites, the electrode was incubated with 10μL 0.1% BSA for 1h and washed thoroughly. Then, 10μL of different concentrations of insulin antigen (Ag) was dropped onto the BSA blocked electrode at 37°C to incubate for 1h and washed with water. After the immunoreactions between Ab1 and Ag, the electrode was labeled by 10μL of PDA@CNT@Ab2 solution incubating for 1h and washed thoroughly. Finally, the PEC immunosensor was well-fabricated ( Scheme 1).

From what had been discussed above, during the photocurrent detection process PDA was served as electron donor. Photocurrent signals were detected on a PEC workstation at room temperature in pH 7.0 PBS with the excitation LED light wavelength of 430nm. The power is 180W and the applied potential of 0V.

For sandwich sensor the label was very important. Fig. 1A and B showed the SEM images of pure CNTs and PDA@CNT respectively. Compared with the pure CNTs under the same magnification, the diameter of PDA@CNT (Fig. 1B) became bigger, indicating that the CNTs have been enwrapped by PDA film. The FTIR images (Fig. 1C) could prove the existence of the PDA@CNT very well. The PDA@CNT presented absorption features: around 1615cm−1 and 1460cm−1 of aromatic rings, 3420cm−1 and 3100cm−1 from catechol -OH groups. Meanwhile, the PDA owned a few intense absorptions at 1460cm−1 and 3100cm−1 respectively. While the CNTs gave absorption at 1615, 1460 and 3420cm−1, which indicated that the graphene structure had affinity to PDA aromatic rings. These features showed that the CNTs were tightly wrapped with PDA film, which was the same as the literature (Fan et al., 2008).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM Characterization of the ITO/WO3/CdS/PDA electrode
ce:section.ce:para: For the application in PEC sensing, the substrate materials would be desirable. Fig. 2 displayed the SEM images of the ITO electrodes modified by the substrate materials. Fig. 2A showed the smooth surface of the as-synthesized WO3 nanorods which could offer more sites for CdS nanoparticles (NPs). Then the electrodes dipped into [Cd(NH3)4]2+ and Na2S aqueous solution respectively forming the craggy surfaces (Fig. 2B), which offering more sites to bind PDA. As depicted in Fig. 2C, after dipping in dopamine Tris-HCl solution, there were many PDA crystals tightly scattered on the surface of the electrodes. The diameter of the electrodes became bigger obviously. Meanwhile the Fig. 2D confirmed the W, O, Cd, and S elementary composition of WO3/CdS electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization of WO3 and CdS
ce:section.ce:para: XPS analysis was usually used to further analyze the surface chemical composition of the materials. Fig. 3A showed the XPS image of WO3 sample. W O and C were observed and the corresponding binding energy of 35.1 (W 4f), 255 (W 4d), 285 (C 1s), 423, 490 (W 4p), and 530eV (O 1s), respectively. The obvious binding energy of 405 (Cd 3d), 617 (Cd 3p), 163 (S 2p) and 229eV (S 2s) could be observed from Fig. 3B. This result was also consistent with the EDS measurement results. The results could well prove the existence of WO3 and CdS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of PEC detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of obtaining the best performance of the PEC sensor, many experiment conditions should be optimized. It could be seen from Fig. 4A, with the increase concentrations of the WO3 from 1.0 to 3.5mgmL−1, the photocurrent intensities were first increased and then decreased, and achieved a maximum value at 2.5mgmL−1. It could be explained that at low concentrations, WO3 could increase the electron conductibility and the contact area, yet at high concentrations, WO3 electron conductibility was not enough to significantly counteract the layer resistance of the sheets. Thus 2.5mgmL−1 was selected during the whole process.

The thickness of CdS film assembled on ITO/WO3 electrode could be adjusted by the coating numbers of CdS NPs. Fig. 4B exhibited the photocurrent of the ITO/WO3/CdS electrodes obtaining undergoing different coating numbers of CdS NPs. With the coating numbers increased, the color of the electrode changed from pale yellow to dark yellow. It indicated that the amount of CdS increased gradually, and caused more and more light absorption, finally the photocurrent intensities increased. It could be clearly seen that after 15 coating numbers the electrode obtained the highest photocurrent intensity. With the coating number further increased, the surface recombination centers were formed on excess CdS, and thus the photocurrent response decreased. Accordingly, 15 coating numbers was choosing during the PEC experiment.

For PEC immunosensor, the optimal value of the pH was also important. As shown in Fig. 4C, it achieved a maximum value at pH 7.0, because detecting in overly acidic or alkaline environment might damage the activity of the immobilized protein (Yuan et al., 2004). Hence, the pH 7.0 PBS was selected for the immunosensor.

Photocurrent responses as showed in Fig. 5A could monitor the succession process of sandwich immunosensor. The ITO electrode and the ITO/WO3 electrode showed small photocurrent intensities (curve a, b). After the electrode was modified by CdS (curve c), the current increased to about 11 times compared to the ITO/WO3 electrode. This was due to the WO3 nanorods owned a large surface area for more CdS deposition, and the CdS significantly inhibited the electron-hole recombination. CdS had suitable band edges, which matched well with WO3 to form a heterojunction. Due to the lower CB level of WO3, electrons could be injected from the CB of CdS to the CB of WO3 under visible irradiation to enhance the photocurrent (Fig. 5C). After the PDA films were subsequently coated (curve d), the photocurrent increased to 15 times higher than that of the ITO/WO3 electrode. The result showed that PDA played a big role in promoting the photo-generated electron-hole separation of the semiconductors. After the continuous immobilization of the Ab1 and BSA, the photocurrent intensity decreased (curve e and f). This was due to the steric hindrance of the protein molecules. When the electrode was incubated with insulin Ag (curve g), the photocurrent intensity decreased to ∼40.5% of the initial sensing electrode. But when the PDA@CNT@Ab2 was attached to the sensor (curve h), the current intensity increased rapidly. This might be contributed to the electrical conductivity of CNT and the effect of PDA.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used as an effective tool to monitor the fabrication process of the immunosensor (Fan et al., 2016; Jiang et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2014b). After the electrodes were fabricated by different steps the impedance spectra were exhibited in Fig. 5B. It included a semicircle portion at higher frequency region equals to electron transfer resistance (R
et ) and a linear portion at lower frequency region corresponds to diffusion limited process (Gao et al., 2014; Li et al., 2012; Lin et al., 2009; Zhu et al., 2016a). The impedance spectrum of bare ITO electrode showed a very small semicircle, which indicated a very small R
et (curve a). When decorated by WO3 and CdS proceed orderly, R
et was much bigger (curve b and c), which indicated the redox probe was hindered access to the electrode surface by the compact film (Li et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). But when the PDA film coated on the electrode, the diameter decreased (curve d). This was attributing to the charge transfer of the PDA. R
et gradually increased when the Ab1 (curve e), BSA (curve f) and Ag (curve g) fixed on the electrode, which due to their insulating effect. When the electrode was incubated with PDA@CNT@Ab2, R
et diameter decreased largely. The entire above phenomenon showed the fabrication process of the immunosensor successfully.

The detection of insulin was based upon the immunoreactions between Ab1 and Ag as well as Ag and PDA@CNT@Ab2. PEC measurement was carried out by a three-electrode system (a Pt wire as counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode and WO3/CdS/PDA modified ITO electrode as working electrode) in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH=7.0). Photocurrent was measured by the current-time curve experimental technique on a PEC workstation with a bias voltage of 0.0V. The wavelength of excitation light was 430nm and the light intensity was 180Wcm−2. The photocurrent response and the concentration of Ag had a directly relationship. Thus, by monitoring the photocurrent changes generated from the sandwich transducer, insulin could be detected. The photocurrent responses of different concentrations of target Ag were presented in Fig. 5D. As the concentrations of target Ag increased, a greater amount of the PDA@CNT@Ab2 conjugates were specifically attached to the electrode surface to accelerate the electron transfer. As a result, it leaded to the gradually increased photocurrent intensities. The photocurrent was linearly increased with an increasing logarithm of the target Ag from 0.01ngmL−1 to 50ngmL−1. The calibration equation was ΔI=12.35+3.75logc (R=0.997), with the limit of detection of 2.8pgmL−1, which was much lower than some reported methods (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Specificity was very important for the immunosensor, since the nonspecific adsorption can influence the accuracy of the detection results. Different potential interferences including glucose, glycine, phenylalanine and histidine were studied under the same condition. The specificity was evaluated by measuring the photocurrent of 1ngmL−1 insulin containing 100ngmL−1 of interfering substance, respectively. The result shown in Fig. S1A, indicating that the current variation was 3.4% displayed an acceptable selectivity.

Five electrodes were prepared under the same conditions for the detection of 1.0ngmL−1 insulin to examine the reproducibility of the immunosensor. The photocurrent were 12.67μA, 12.61μA, 13.25μA, 12.71μA and 13.29μA, respectively with the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.6%, suggesting that the reproducibility of immunosensors were fairly satisfactory. The results indicated that the proposed PEC transducer was repeatable and had an acceptable accuracy.

The stability of the proposed immunosensor was also investigated and displayed in Fig. S1B. There were no noticeable changes of photocurrent response when recorded under several on/off cycles which proved the good storage stability.

In this study, a sandwich PEC transducer for insulin detection was designed successfully based on WO3/CdS/PDA co-sensitized structure as matrix and PDA@CNT conjugates as signal labels. The PDA modified WO3/CdS electrode could reduce the toxicity of CdS and enhance the photocurrent. As for the WO3/CdS/PDA co-sensitized electrode, the significantly enhanced photocurrent intensity was achieved due to its excellent properties of adequate light absorption ability, ultrafast electron transfer efficiency as well as the capability to inhibit the charge recombination. The PDA@CNT conjugates, as signal amplification labels, could significantly enhance the photocurrent intensity due to the fast electron transfer. In addition, the proposed method had been applied for the detection of insulin in real serum sample successfully. Moreover, the enhanced co-sensitization system could also be extended to other important biological interactions such as enzyme immunosensor or cell analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575050, 21627809, 21405059, 21377046 and 21505051), the Science and Technology Plan Project of Jinan (No. 201307010), the Special Project for Independent Innovation and Achievements Transformation of Shandong Province (2014ZZCX05101), and QW thanks the Special Foundation for Taishan Scholar Professorship of Shandong Province and UJN (No. ts20130937), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M612170), Doctoral and Postdoctoral Science Foundation of University of Jinan (No. XBS1658), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M590609).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.05.029.
